perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees therefore that Discloser will have the right to seek, in addition to any of its other rights and remedies under law and equity, injunctive relief for any violation of this NDA without posting bond or by posting bond at the lowest amount required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees therefore that Discloser will have the right to seek, in addition to any of its other rights and remedies under law and equity, injunctive relief for any violation of this NDA by posting bond.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the option for Discloser to seek injunctive relief 'without posting bond' contradicts Ontario law, which allows courts discretion in waiving bond requirements in certain circumstances, particularly where irreparable harm is demonstrated and the bond poses an undue burden.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario Courts of Justice Act, RSO 1990, c C.43, Rule 40.03
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 40.03 of the Ontario Courts of Justice Act grants the court discretion regarding bond requirements for injunctions. This modification eliminates the 'without posting bond' option, potentially contradicting the court's ability to waive the bond in appropriate cases, thereby restricting the Discloser's legal rights and remedies under Ontario law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract allows the Discloser to seek injunctive relief 'without posting bond,' which aligns with the Ontario Courts of Justice Act's discretion regarding bond requirements. The modified text removes this option, mandating that the Discloser must post a bond. The Law Explanation states that this change 'potentially contradicting the court's ability to waive the bond in appropriate cases, thereby restricting the Discloser's legal rights and remedies under Ontario law.' Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Rule 40.03 concerning 'Undertaking' in relation to 'Interlocutory Injunction or Mandatory Order', supports the premise that bond requirements are subject to court discretion. Removing the 'without posting bond' option directly contradicts this discretion, making the contract more restrictive than what is permitted under Ontario law. Therefore, the omission creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This NDA will automatically renew for subsequent one (1) year periods on the anniversary of the Effective Date, unless otherwise terminated in writing by either party.
perturbation.changed_text: This NDA will automatically renew for subsequent one (1) year periods on the anniversary of the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'unless otherwise terminated in writing by either party' creates an automatic, perpetual renewal. This could violate the principle of contract law requiring mutual consent for contractual obligations and might be seen as an unfair or unconscionable term, particularly if one party no longer wishes to be bound but has no explicit mechanism for termination.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles of Contract Formation and Termination in Ontario
perturbation.law_explanation: The removal of the termination clause creates an automatic renewal with no possibility of written termination, which could run contrary to contract law's requirements for mutual consent and fair dealing, potentially rendering the automatic renewal provision unenforceable under Ontario law.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the termination clause creates a contradiction. The original contract allowed for termination in writing by either party, ensuring mutual consent. The changed text removes this option, potentially creating a perpetual, automatic renewal that may not reflect the ongoing consent of both parties. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses compensation for victims of crime and doesn't directly address contract termination, the Perturbation and Law Explanations highlight the core issue: contract law requires mutual consent, and the removal of the termination clause undermines this principle, potentially leading to an unenforceable automatic renewal.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall protect the Confidential Information using the same degree of care that Recipient uses to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature to prevent the unauthorized use, reproduction or disclosure of the Confidential Information. Such care shall not be less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall protect the Confidential Information using the same degree of care that Recipient uses to protect its own confidential information to prevent the unauthorized use, reproduction or disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'of a similar nature' and 'Such care shall not be less than a reasonable degree of care,' the clause becomes ambiguous and unenforceable. Without a minimum standard of care (reasonable degree), the Recipient could argue that even minimal efforts suffice, even if their own confidential information receives much greater protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Duty of Care in Contractual Obligations (Ontario)
perturbation.law_citation: Duty of Care in Contract Law (Ontario)
perturbation.law_explanation: The removal of the 'reasonable degree of care' standard weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially violating the common law duty of care implied in contractual relationships. The 'reasonable degree of care' sets a minimum standard and failure to meet that can lead to breach of duty, but by taking it out and only comparing it to how they treat their own information, it can open it up to not being upheld in court.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Canada may still be far from CBD legalization | Canadian Lawyer CONTINUE TO SITE CONTINUE TO SITE Canada may still be far from CBD legalization Those looking to break into the cannabis market with cannabinol products may have their hands tied for the foreseeable future as some lawyers predict a lengthy delay in CBD legalization. Lewis Retik, partner at Gowling WLG, says that it hard for him to see CBD legalization becoming a priority for Health Canada in the near future. By Julia Nowicki 13 Feb 2019 Share Those looking to break into the cannabis market with cannabinol products may have their hands tied for the foreseeable future as some lawyers predict a lengthy delay in CBD legalization. During a panel discussion hosted by the Ontario Bar Association last week, Lewis Retik, a partner at Gowling WLG, said that although the Cannabis Act opened the door to the legal recreational cannabis market in Canada, the use of cannabinoids in over-the-counter products, such as cosmetics, is still prohibited without a licence. In 2018, the Cannabis Act removed cannabis from the Controlled Drug Substances Act and now regulates its production, distribution and sale through rigid market regulations. However, CBD, a phytocannabinoid present in cannabis and thought to have little psychoactive effects, continues to be listed under the Prescription Drug List. Retik said that although this classification effectively prohibited them from being considered a natural health product, Health Canada took an extra step and specifically exempted them from the Natural Health Products Act in 2018. In order to change the classification of CBD as an over-the-counter or natural health product, not only must an application be filed for a switch that proves the compound can be safely regulated in a different classification, Retik said, Health Canada must also amend the NPH regulations. Given the momentous delays the federal government is experiencing in similar requests, the wait time could be anywhere around five, six or seven years, absent political intervention, Retik said. “The practical reality of it is, I feel like it’s going to be a long shot,” Retik said. “While it’s possible they are going to amend the natural health product regulations to remove CBD from the exclusion factors, that doesn’t happen often and it’s not an easy thing to do.” Trina Fraser, co-managing partner at Brazeau Seller Law and head of their CannaLaw group, also sat as a panelist and said that she is a little more optimistic about the future of CBD products’ entry into the legal market. “There is such a pervasive, illicit market of CBD products that it puts pressure on the regulator,” Fraser said. “There is obviously a very high level of demand for these products and when you don’t provide the channels for the legal sale of products that are in high demand, guess what, the illicit market will step in and fill it for you.” Fraser said that the World Health Organization has recently recommended de-scheduling CBD because “it’s not intoxicating” and has very little or no potential for addiction or problematic use. Peter Simeon, partner at Gowling WLG, said that the movement of the CBD and hemp industry in Europe and the U.S. may influence the Canadian government to make similar changes. “We’ve seen an explosion of the CBD market in the U.S. and in other countries, and everybody kind of envisioned the same excitement for CBD here in Canada,” Retik says. “It’s just been a little bit curtailed because we don’t have access to over the counter sales like natural health products would have allowed us to do, so that begs the question of if and when the federal government will revisit that.” The Canadian government has proposed a plan to regulate the licensed sale of other cannabis products including edibles, drinks, extracts and topicals, which is expected to come into force by Oct. 17, 2019. Products will be subject to specific guidelines that limit the amount of THC, a psychoactive component of
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the obligation to protect confidential information by removing the 'reasonable degree of care' standard. The changed text omits a legally implied duty of care, potentially leading to non-compliance. The explanation is valid because without a reasonable standard the recipient can argue they took steps to protect the data, even if they were minimal. The first snippet is irrelevant, but the second snippet references that the 'Cannabis Act opened the door to the legal recreational cannabis market in Canada, the use of cannabinoids in over-the-counter products, such as cosmetics, is still prohibited without a licence' which shows how there is some obligation to protect consumer data, and there can be serious legal ramifications if data is not properly protected.
